description: Phrases that should never appear in SoW documents as they create inappropriate commitments or ambiguity

bannedPhrases.reason: Creates ambiguous commitment without clear deliverable criteria
bannedPhrases.suggestion: Use 'commercially reasonable efforts' with specific success criteria, or remove entirely
Avoid guaranteeing outcomes; focus on deliverables and process
Use 'will' or 'intends to' instead, focus on process not outcomes

bannedPhrases.reason: Gives unilateral decision-making authority without criteria
bannedPhrases.suggestion: Specify criteria for decisions or remove
Use 'will use commercially reasonable efforts to' or simply 'will'

bannedPhrases.reason: Subjective acceptance criteria without objective measures
bannedPhrases.suggestion: Provide specific, measurable, objective acceptance criteria
Define specific scope, frequency, or trigger conditions
Use specific ranges or estimates with context
Focus on deliverables and activities, not outcomes

bannedPhrases.reason: Implies additional qualification or certification, creates warranty risk that these people are special
bannedPhrases.suggestion: Use 'knowledgeable individual', 'experienced professional', or 'subject matter specialist'

bannedPhrases.reason: Highest possible standard poses potential warranty issue
bannedPhrases.suggestion: Use 'commercially reasonable efforts' or 'industry standard quality'

bannedPhrases.reason: Legal phrase meaning both parties understand time is critically important; avoid unless intentional
bannedPhrases.suggestion: Use 'timely delivery is important' or specify milestone dates with flexibility

bannedPhrases.reason: SLAs should be avoided whenever possible; requires baselining existing levels and creates hard commitments
bannedPhrases.suggestion: Describe expected service characteristics without creating an SLA commitment, or work with Contract Executive

bannedPhrases.reason: SLA abbreviation should be avoided; creates service level commitments
bannedPhrases.suggestion: Describe service expectations without SLA terminology

additionalPatterns.description: Any form of 'guarantee' should be avoided
Any reference to unlimited scope or quantity
Subjective satisfaction criteria should have objective measures
Specific delivery dates should be avoided as they can change based on contract signing
additionalPatterns.suggestion: Use 'estimated timeframes' or 'within X weeks of project kickoff'

additionalPatterns.description: The term 'expert' implies special qualification and creates warranty risk
additionalPatterns.suggestion: Use 'knowledgeable professional', 'experienced team member', or 'subject matter specialist'

additionalPatterns.description: Superlatives about quality create potential warranty issues
